title: Global PreToolUse hook to enforce worktree discipline, with per-repo opt-out
description:
# Body

## What
Add a global PreToolUse hook to bento that blocks `Edit`, `Write`, and `NotebookEdit` tool calls when the agent is operating directly on `main` without a worktree. The default is enforce; repos opt out by creating `.agent-mode.local` with `require_worktree=false`.

The hook is registered in the user-global Claude Code `settings.json` (`~/.claude/settings.json`) as a `hooks.PreToolUse` entry pointing to a shell script shipped with bento.

## Why
Agents routinely skip `bento:launch-work` — the skill is a behavioral instruction and can be overlooked. A hook enforces the same contract mechanically, at the tool layer, before any file is written.

## Behavior

**Default (no `.agent-mode.local`, or file present but `require_worktree` key absent):** hook fires before `Edit`/`Write`/`NotebookEdit`, runs `git branch --show-current`, and exits non-zero if the result is `main`. The error message tells the agent exactly how to opt out:

```
Blocked: editing files directly on 'main' is not allowed.
To disable this check for this repo, add 'require_worktree=false' to .agent-mode.local.
```

**Opt-out:** create `.agent-mode.local` in the repo root with the line `require_worktree=false`. The file is parsed as `key=value` (one per line, `#` comments ignored). If the file exists but does not contain `require_worktree=false`, the hook enforces (fail-safe default). Adding `.agent-mode.local` to `.gitignore` is a manual step by the developer; the hook does not modify `.gitignore`.

**No-op cases:**
- Not in a git repo (git command fails or exits non-zero): allow.
- Detached HEAD (`git branch --show-current` returns empty string): allow.
- Any branch other than `main`: allow.
- `.agent-mode.local` present with `require_worktree=false`: allow.

**V1 scope note:** default branch is hard-coded to `main`. No configurability for alternate default branch names in v1.

## Acceptance checks
- [ ] Hook is registered in `~/.claude/settings.json` as a `hooks.PreToolUse` entry pointing to the bento-shipped script.
- [ ] `Edit` on a file while on `main` in a git repo (no `.agent-mode.local`) exits non-zero with the specified error message.
- [ ] `Write` on a file while on `main` is blocked.
- [ ] `NotebookEdit` on a file while on `main` is blocked.
- [ ] A repo with `.agent-mode.local` containing `require_worktree=false` passes all three tool calls through unblocked.
- [ ] A repo with `.agent-mode.local` that does NOT contain `require_worktree=false` (key absent or other value) is still blocked.
- [ ] A non-git directory passes all three tool calls through unblocked.
- [ ] A detached HEAD state passes all three tool calls through unblocked.
- [ ] Any non-main branch (e.g., `pp-abc`) passes all three tool calls through unblocked.

## Out of scope (v1)
- Checking for a linked Beads issue before allowing edits.
- Validating worktree path conventions.
- Blocking `Bash` write commands.
- Configurable default branch name.
- Checked-in (per-repo, committed) opt-out — opt-out is intentionally developer-local.
- Hook auto-updating `.gitignore` with `.agent-mode.local`.

title: Teach issue precheck to request Codex subagent permission
description:
# Teach issue precheck to request Codex subagent permission

## Body

Codex runtimes cannot launch subagents unless the current user request explicitly authorizes delegation. The issue-completeness-precheck skill currently says Codex should spawn a fresh reviewer only when permission was already granted, otherwise use local fallback for normal drafts and ask only for broad, high-risk, ambiguous, or subtle repro issues.

This means ordinary Codex issue filing often downgrades to local self-review even when a fresh reviewer would be better. Update the canonical issue-completeness-precheck skill so Codex instances proactively ask the user for permission to launch a fresh reviewer whenever delegation is available but not yet authorized, then spawn the subagent if the user grants permission. Keep local fallback only for cases where delegation is unavailable or the user declines.

Acceptance checks:
- The Codex section clearly instructs agents to ask for one-time permission to delegate the fresh-reviewer precheck when delegation is available but not authorized.
- The workflow makes clear that, after permission is granted, Codex should spawn the reviewer with only the draft path and review path.
- Local fallback remains documented for delegation-unavailable or user-declined cases, and records `review_mode: local-fallback`.
- Generated plugin artifacts and marketplace metadata are rebuilt from the canonical skill source.
- Version bump workflow is evaluated, and a behavioral version bump is applied if warranted.

In scope:
- `catalog/skills/issue-completeness-precheck/SKILL.md`
- generated plugin output and manifests produced by `scripts/build-plugins`
- plugin version metadata if the version bump workflow classifies this as behavioral

Out of scope:
- changing Codex runtime authorization rules
- changing tracker filing semantics outside the precheck workflow

title: launch-work: scan for existing issues before creating a new one
description:
**Description:**

When `launch-work` is invoked and the repo uses a tracker (Beads or GitHub Issues), the agent currently has no explicit guidance to look for an existing issue before deciding to create one. This leads to duplicate tracker items when similar work has already been filed.

**Change:** In step 2 of the `launch-work` workflow, replace the passive "if the work is not tracker-backed, record that explicitly and continue" with active guidance:
- If the repo uses a tracker and no issue is already identified for the current work, scan open issues for a close match using the tracker's list/search commands.
- If a matching issue is found, use it (claim it per the repo's active-work policy).
- If no issue matches well, create a new one via the tracker skill's filing flow (including the pre-filing review step), then claim it.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- `SKILL.md` step 2 instructs the agent to scan first, reuse if matched, create if not.
- The existing flow for already-linked issues (Beads / GitHub Issues / not-tracker-backed) is preserved.
- No other steps change.

**In scope:** Edit `catalog/skills/launch-work/SKILL.md` step 2 only.
**Out of scope:** Changes to tracker skill files, helper scripts, or other workflow steps.

**Size:** XS — single paragraph edit in one file.

title: Add pre-filing readiness check to issue-flow skills
description: Filing agents currently submit issues whose ambiguity/scope problems only surface at work time. Add a Pre-Filing Readiness Check section to both beads-issue-flow and github-issue-flow: drafter writes issue body to a local temp file, dispatches a fresh subagent to assess readiness from that file alone, subagent writes verdict to a sibling temp file. Only after the draft passes (or is explicitly tagged triage-needed) does it get submitted via bd create / gh issue create. Handoff via temp file ensures unworkable drafts never enter the tracker.

title: Fix land-work vs closure skill triggers
description:
Agents finishing work on a worktree pick closure for cleanup instead of land-work.

Root cause: skill descriptions point the wrong way.

1. land-work description does not advertise post-merge cleanup. It says 'land via command line — verify preconditions, check the landing lease, merge, and close tracker work.' Nothing about removing the worktree or branch. An agent thinking 'clean up my worktree' has no trigger to match, even though land-work step 10 IS the prescribed cleanup path.

2. closure description leads with 'garbage-collection... leftover git state... worktrees' which exactly matches what a finishing agent thinks it is doing. The 'Not for cleaning up your own active or just-finished work; use land-work for that' carve-out comes second; positive matches win over negative carve-outs.

Fix:
- Rewrite land-work description to surface the full lifecycle including post-merge worktree and branch teardown.
- Rewrite closure description to lead with the disqualifier and avoid language a finishing agent would self-apply.
- Optionally add a hard-trigger line on land-work parallel to launch-work's pattern.

title: Design: extension points for launch-work and land-work (script + prose actions, pre/main/post)
description: Design spec for adding pre/main/post extension points to launch-work and land-work, supporting both executable script hooks (existing, expanded) and plain-language prose actions (new). See spec written to docs/specs/.
close_reason: Landed via merge e60951a on main.

title: Clarify same-session cleanup guidance between land-work and closure
description:
The land-work skill currently instructs agents to run closure in single-target mode after landing a feature branch, while the closure skill says not to use closure for the caller's own just-finished work and says land-work should own that cleanup. This contradiction led to an agent invoking closure-scan on its own recently-active feature worktree after landing, which safely skipped but was unnecessary and confusing.

Proposed direction:
- Update closure language to explicitly say single-target mode is for later cleanup passes over other agents/previous sessions, not same-session cleanup of the caller's own branch.
- Update land-work post-merge cleanup language to avoid saying to hand off to closure for the caller's own branch.
- If a landing-owned cleanup helper exists, name it directly; otherwise instruct agents to leave the feature branch/worktree in place and report the path/name.

Acceptance criteria:
- land-work and closure no longer give contradictory instructions for just-landed same-session work.
- closure docs explicitly mention that --target-branch does not override the same-session prohibition.
- land-work docs describe the supported cleanup behavior without telling agents to invoke the closure skill for their own active worktree.

Push to origin failed: git@github.com Permission denied (publickey). Issue intentionally left in_progress until landed.

title: generate-audit: add cognitive complexity as a cross-language metric (gocognit, clippy, eslint-sonarjs, PMD)
description:
## Gap

`catalog/skills/generate-audit/` recommends `gocyclo` (cyclomatic complexity) for Go and nothing equivalent for other languages. Cognitive complexity is a different metric and a useful complement; the skill omits it entirely.

## The two metrics

- **Cyclomatic** (gocyclo, ESLint `complexity`, RuboCop `Metrics/CyclomaticComplexity`): counts independent paths. Every `if`, `for`, `case`, `&&`, `||` adds 1. Reproducible. Insensitive to nesting depth.
- **Cognitive** (SonarSource, 2018 white paper): weights *how hard the code is to understand*. Nesting compounds. Early returns don't add. Mixed boolean operators add. Linear chains of guard clauses don't.

A flat function with twelve `if err != nil { return err }` blocks has cyclomatic 12, cognitive ~1. A function with three nested loops + one condition has cyclomatic 4, cognitive 7. They catch different shapes; running both is the standard practice in repos that care about either.

## Per-language tool mapping (broadly applicable: most languages have a tool)

| Language | Tool | Run |
|---|---|---|
| Go | `gocognit` | `gocognit -over 15 .` (or via golangci-lint's `gocognit` linter) |
| Rust | `clippy::cognitive_complexity` | Default-allow; enable via `#![warn(clippy::cognitive_complexity)]` or `clippy.toml`; threshold knob |
| TypeScript / JavaScript | `eslint-plugin-sonarjs` rule `sonarjs/cognitive-complexity` | ESLint config; threshold knob |
| Java | PMD `CognitiveComplexity` rule, or SonarQube/SonarLint native | Maven/Gradle plugin |
| Python | `flake8-cognitive-complexity` plugin | flake8 config |

Cross-language alternative for polyglot repos: `lizard` (Python-based, cyclomatic by default, length + parameter count too). Single tool, many languages, no per-language config — useful when the per-language story would be fragmented.

## Suggested fix

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md`:

1. Add per-language entries for each tool above. Severity: cognitive > 15 → `warning`, > 30 → `error`. Threshold knobs documented per tool.
2. Note that cognitive complexity complements (not replaces) cyclomatic complexity. Both are recommended where available.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/scripts/audit-discover.py`:

3. Per-language tool detection in `_TOOL_PROBES` for each of gocognit, eslint-plugin-sonarjs (config detection), PMD (build-tool integration), clippy (Cargo.toml + clippy.toml).
4. Optionally add `lizard` as a cross-language fallback when no per-language tool is detected and the repo is polyglot.

## Caveats worth surfacing in the recommendation

- Cognitive complexity has more interpretation calls than cyclomatic; same code can score slightly differently across tool versions and edge cases (early-return weighting, mixed-operator counting). Pin tool versions in CI.
- Recommending both metrics means maintainers learn two scales. The skill should propose default thresholds, not require teams to invent them.

## Acceptance

A regenerated audit for a Go repo emits gocognit alongside gocyclo. A regenerated audit for a Rust/TS/Java repo emits the corresponding cognitive-complexity tool. A regenerated audit for a polyglot repo without per-language tools surfaces `lizard` as a fallback.

## Refute counterpart

Wait until gocognit recommendation lands in golangci-lint config (per `bento-4k6` and refute issue #13). Then the refute-side adoption is just enabling `gocognit` in `.golangci.yml`, no separate work.

title: generate-audit: recommend depcheck for TypeScript/JavaScript dependency hygiene
description:
## Gap

`catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md` recommends `knip` for unused exports in TypeScript/JavaScript. It does not recommend `depcheck`.

`knip` and `depcheck` solve adjacent but different problems:

- `knip`: unused **exports** (code declared but not imported anywhere)
- `depcheck`: unused **dependencies** (entries in `package.json` not imported anywhere) and **missing dependencies** (imports of packages not declared in `package.json`)

A `package.json` accumulates dead deps as features come and go. A `package.json` that imports something not declared works locally (transitive resolution) but breaks for downstream consumers.

## Why broadly applicable

Every TypeScript or JavaScript repo has a `package.json`. The drift between declared and used deps is universal — not narrow.

## Suggested fix

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md`:

1. Add `### depcheck` entry. Run: `npx depcheck`. Severity: missing dep used in code → `error`; unused declared dep → `note` (review for dynamic loads before reporting).
2. Note that `depcheck` and `knip` are complementary, not alternatives.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/scripts/audit-discover.py`:

3. Add `depcheck` to TypeScript/JavaScript tool list alongside `knip`.

## Acceptance

A regenerated audit for any TS/JS repo emits both `knip` (unused exports) and `depcheck` (unused/missing deps) recommendations.

## Refute counterpart

Will file a separate refute issue against `adapters/tsmorph/` since that package has no test/lint scripts and likely has dep drift.

title: generate-audit: recommend actionlint for any repo with .github/workflows/
description:
## Gap

`catalog/skills/generate-audit/` has no entry for `actionlint`. The skill recommends adding CI workflows on repos that lack them but doesn't recommend linting workflows that exist.

## Why broadly applicable

Every GitHub-using repo accumulates GitHub Actions workflows. Every workflow file accumulates errors that GitHub's UI hides until they fire: shellcheck'd `run:` blocks, deprecated action versions, undefined variable references in expressions, mismatched `needs:` graphs, OS-specific syntax used cross-platform.

`actionlint` catches all of the above, runs in seconds, has near-zero false positives. It's the workflow equivalent of `gofmt` — bewildering not to use once you've adopted the language.

## Suggested fix

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md`:

1. Add `### actionlint` entry. Run: `actionlint`. Severity: any error → `warning` (workflow bugs that fire in CI are noisy but rarely catastrophic); shellcheck-class issues in `run:` blocks → match shellcheck severity.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/scripts/audit-discover.py`:

2. Detect `.github/workflows/*.yml`. When present, add `actionlint` to detected/applicable tools. When absent, no recommendation (no workflows to lint).

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/SKILL.md`:

3. When the audit recommends adding CI (current behavior for repos without workflows), include `actionlint` in the proposed setup so the new workflows are linted from day one.

## Acceptance

A regenerated audit for any repo with `.github/workflows/` emits an `actionlint` recommendation. A regenerated audit for a repo without workflows emits the existing CI-baseline recommendation augmented with actionlint setup instructions.

## Note

Refute has no `.github/workflows/` today. The corresponding refute issue is the existing CI-baseline ask, not a separate actionlint issue.

title: generate-audit: recommend osv-scanner as the primary cross-language vuln scanner
description:
## Gap

`catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md` recommends `govulncheck` for Go and `nancy` for Go module deps. It does not recommend `osv-scanner`.

`osv-scanner` (Google) reads `go.mod`, `package-lock.json`, `Cargo.lock`, `requirements.txt`, `pom.xml`, `Gemfile.lock`, and SBOMs in one pass against the OSV database. Single tool, every language the audit might encounter.

## Why broadly applicable

Every repo has dependencies. Every repo's deps eventually have CVEs. A cross-language scanner that takes one config and returns one normalized output beats N per-language scanners on every multi-language repo and ties on single-language ones.

`osv-scanner` is well-maintained, fast, and produces SARIF output that integrates with GitHub Advanced Security and other consumers. It supersedes `nancy` cleanly. It's complementary to `govulncheck` (which adds reachability analysis on Go-only) but for a generalist audit, `osv-scanner` is the right default.

## Suggested fix

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md`:

1. Add `### osv-scanner` entry. Run: `osv-scanner --recursive .`. Severity: any unreached vuln → `warning`; reached (when reachability is determinable) → `error`.
2. Per the highest-value-per-gap rule in `generation-rules.md`, recommend `osv-scanner` instead of `nancy` and as a peer to `govulncheck` (Go reachability) for Go-only repos.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/scripts/audit-discover.py`:

3. Add `osv-scanner` to the cross-language tool list. Detection: `shutil.which("osv-scanner")` or presence of `osv-scanner.toml`.

## Acceptance

A regenerated audit for any repo with deps emits an `osv-scanner` recommendation. For Go-only repos, the recommendation is paired with `govulncheck` for reachability. `nancy` is no longer recommended.

## Refute counterpart

Will file a separate refute issue to actually run `osv-scanner` and address findings.

title: generate-audit: detect input→output repos and surface golden-file test harness as a coverage gap
description:
## Gap

`catalog/skills/generate-audit/` measures coverage by line, package, risk surface. It does not detect when a repo's purpose is **input → output transformation** and ask whether a golden-file regression harness exists. For tools that compile, format, refactor, transpile, generate, lint, or translate, this is the strongest possible test signal — and no line-coverage metric surfaces its absence.

## Why it matters

Real audit (refute, 2026-05-02). Refute's entire purpose is "given source A, produce source B." It has input fixtures (`testdata/fixtures/{go,rust,javascript,typescript}/rename/`) but no expected-output fixtures and no diff-against-golden harness. Current integration tests do `strings.Contains(content, "BuildGreeting")` assertions — they catch "did the rename happen?" but not "did *only* the rename happen?" An LSP server that rewrites an unrelated file (auto-formatting, import re-ordering, etc.) goes undetected.

This is a substantial test-quality gap that the audit's coverage module did not surface because the affected lines *are* covered, just not strongly.

## Not a library recommendation

The audit should not say "use cupaloy" or "use goldie." Five competing Go libraries exist; most teams hand-roll their own with `cmp.Diff`. The skill's job is to detect the missing **pattern**, not the missing dependency.

## Suggested fix

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/scripts/audit-discover.py`:

1. Detect input→output project signal: presence of `testdata/fixtures/`, `testdata/inputs/`, `testdata/cases/`, or similar. Combined with: a CLI entrypoint, OR a public API whose primary signature transforms structured input to structured output (e.g., `func Transform(src []byte) ([]byte, error)`).
2. Detect existing golden-file harness signal: presence of `testdata/golden/`, `testdata/expected/`, `testdata/want/`, or test files containing `goldie`, `cupaloy`, `*.golden`, `*.want`, `*-update` flag definitions, or `cmp.Diff`-against-fixture patterns.
3. Emit a finding: input→output project + no golden harness → `warning`-level test-strategy gap, with a worked example tailored to the detected fixture layout.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/SKILL.md` (test-strategy diversity module proposed in `bento-43l`):

4. Add "golden-file regression" as a fourth signal alongside unit / property / fuzz. Absence in an input→output project is a finding distinct from low coverage.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md`:

5. Add a brief `### golden-file harness` reference describing the pattern, the two-stage workflow (`go test` vs `go test -update`), and the "review the diff before committing" review discipline. List Go libraries (cupaloy, goldie, manual `cmp.Diff`) without picking one.

## Refute-specific worked example for the issue body

Refute's natural golden layout:

\`\`\`
testdata/golden/rename-go-function/
  input/...         # tree to copy into t.TempDir()
  cmd.txt           # refute invocation, one per line
  expected/...      # post-rename tree
\`\`\`

Test runs every directory under `testdata/golden/`, copies `input/`, executes `cmd.txt`, diffs against `expected/`. `-update` regenerates `expected/` from current behavior; reviewer scrutinizes the diff in PR.

## Acceptance

A regenerated audit for refute (or any input→output repo without golden tests) emits a "golden-file harness missing" finding with a worked example derived from the repo's existing fixture layout.

title: generate-audit: add property-based testing as a module for pure-function-heavy Go domains
description:
## Gap

`catalog/skills/generate-audit/` recommends fuzzing (per `bento-2g5`) and unit-coverage gap analysis. It does not mention property-based testing. The two tools are commonly confused but have different oracles:

- **Fuzzing**: oracle is "does the program crash?" (panic, hang, OOM).
- **Property-based**: oracle is "does invariant I(f(x)) hold for x drawn from domain D?" — finds correctness bugs that complete cleanly.

Both use generative input + shrinking. Fuzzing is in the skill; property-based is not.

## Why it matters for refute (and similar repos)

Real audit (refute, 2026-05-02). Refute is a refactoring tool; its core operations have crisp invariants the audit explicitly noted but the skill had no module for:

- `applyEdits(content, edits)`: bytes outside ranges unchanged; bytes inside replaced ranges equal new text; length = sum of deltas.
- `applyEdits` round-trip with inverse edits = identity.
- `positionToOffset` start-of-line property.
- `parseWorkspaceEdit` serialize/parse identity.

A property-based suite would have caught https://github.com/shatterproof-ai/refute/issues/3 (Apply is not atomic) directly: the property "either all files have new content or all files have old content" is visibly violated by the implementation.

Refactoring tools, parsers, serializers, math kernels, format converters — these are all property-based-test sweet spots. The skill should recognize the pattern.

## Suggested fix

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md`:

1. Add `### rapid` entry (preferred Go property-based library) and note `gopter` as the older alternative. Not a CLI tool — recommendation is "package X has property-based test coverage" or not.
2. Severity: absence of property-based tests in a package whose public API is pure-function-heavy on byte/string inputs → `warning`.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/scripts/audit-discover.py`:

3. Detect pure-function-heavy candidacy: count public functions with signature `func(...) (T, error)` where args are byte/string/int and T is a value type. High count + zero `pgregory.net/rapid` import → recommendation gap.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/SKILL.md`:

4. Add a "test-strategy diversity" audit module distinct from coverage. Module surfaces three signals per risk-surface package: presence of unit tests, presence of property-based tests, presence of fuzz tests. Absence of any one in a high-risk-surface package is a finding.

## Acceptance

A regenerated audit for a Go repo with parser/serializer/transformation code in risk surfaces emits a `rapid` recommendation listing concrete property candidates derived from the public API.

title: land-work cleanup guidance conflicts
description:
## Problem

`catalog/skills/land-work/SKILL.md` currently directs agents to clean up a just-landed feature branch by handing off to `closure` in single-target mode. `catalog/skills/closure/SKILL.md` says closure is not for routine cleanup of the calling agent's own just-finished work. The conflicting guidance caused an agent to invoke the closure helper after landing its own branch; the helper then skipped deletion because the worktree was recently active.

## Observed behavior

After landing a branch, the agent followed land-work's cleanup step and ran `closure/scripts/closure-scan.py --target-branch <branch> --apply delete-local-merged-branches`. Closure refused to delete the branch/worktree due liveness safety. The correct cleanup was the direct post-landing sequence from the primary checkout: `git worktree remove <worktree-path>` followed by `git branch -d <feature-branch>`.

## Expected behavior

Bento guidance should make the routine post-landing path unambiguous. Agents finishing their own just-landed work should use direct cleanup from the primary checkout. Closure single-target mode should be reserved for stale, ambiguous, or other-agent leftovers, or for diagnostics after direct cleanup fails.

## Suggested fix

Update `catalog/skills/land-work/SKILL.md` step 10 to stop prescribing closure for the active agent's own branch. Replace it with explicit direct cleanup commands and mention closure only as a fallback for stale/ambiguous leftovers.

Update `catalog/skills/closure/SKILL.md` to add a short “Routine post-landing cleanup for your own branch” section and clarify that single-target mode is not the normal just-landed branch cleanup path.

Because these are behavioral skill instructions under `catalog/skills/`, evaluate the required Bento version bump and regenerate plugin artifacts per AGENTS.md.
acceptance_criteria:
`land-work` tells agents to run direct cleanup for their own just-landed branch: `git worktree remove <path>` then `git branch -d <branch>`.

`closure` explicitly says single-target apply mode is for stale/ambiguous/other-agent leftovers or fallback diagnostics, not routine cleanup by the active landing agent.

Generated plugin artifacts are rebuilt if the canonical skill text changes, and version bump workflow is followed if required.

title: generate-audit: enable errorlint in recommended golangci-lint baseline
description:
## Gap

`catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md` recommends installing `golangci-lint` but does not prescribe enabling `errorlint`. The default golangci-lint linter set does not include it.

Real audit (refute, 2026-05-02): the codebase wraps errors heavily — `fmt.Errorf("...: %w", err)` appears at dozens of call sites — and uses `errors.As` in `isLSPError` and `isRetryableRenameError` (`internal/backend/lsp/client.go:58-69`). One mistyped `%s` instead of `%w`, or one `if err == ErrFoo` against a wrapped sentinel, would silently break error classification. No other linter in the default set catches this.

## Why it matters

`errorlint` is the only tool that catches:

1. `if err == sentinel` instead of `errors.Is(err, sentinel)` for sentinels that may be wrapped.
2. `err.(*MyErr)` type assertion instead of `errors.As`.
3. `fmt.Errorf("...: %s", err)` (or `%v`) instead of `%w`.

Drop-in: enable `errorlint` in `.golangci.yml`. Near-zero false positives in practice.

## Suggested fix

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md` golangci-lint entry: add `errorlint` to the recommended baseline `linters: enable:` list. Severity: any errorlint finding → audit `error`.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/scripts/audit-discover.py`: when `% w`-style error wrapping is detected in Go source (count `fmt.Errorf.*%w` matches), gate `errorlint` recommendation as high-priority.

## Acceptance

A regenerated audit for a Go repo with > 10 `%w` wrapping sites includes `errorlint` in its recommended `.golangci.yml` enable list.

## Related

Split out from `bento-nzm` (golangci-lint config bundle).

title: generate-audit: recommend go.uber.org/goleak for Go test packages with long-lived goroutines
description:
## Gap

`catalog/skills/generate-audit/` does not mention `go.uber.org/goleak`. The skill recommends coverage tools and linters but has no test-time goroutine-leak check.

Real audit (refute, 2026-05-02): `internal/backend/lsp/client.go:233` spawns `readLoop` as a goroutine. `Shutdown()` drains it via `<-c.done` then `c.process.Wait()`. If a test exits without calling `Shutdown` (or `Shutdown` errors before `<-c.done`), `readLoop` keeps running against a closed pipe forever. No existing check catches this.

## Why it matters

`goleak.VerifyTestMain(m)` is a 3-line addition per test package. Fails the test if any non-runtime goroutine is still running after `m.Run()`. Different failure mode from `-race`: `-race` catches concurrent-access bugs in code that runs; `goleak` catches goroutines that don't stop.

## Suggested fix

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md`:

1. Add `### goleak` entry. Not a CLI tool — it's a test-time check enabled via `goleak.VerifyTestMain(m)` or `defer goleak.VerifyNone(t)`. Mention both forms.
2. Severity: any leak → audit `error` when running tests; absence in goroutine-heavy packages → `warning`-level recommendation gap.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/scripts/audit-discover.py`:

3. Detect packages spawning goroutines (presence of `go func` or `go someFunc(` in non-test files of a package) and report them in a `goroutine_packages` field.
4. For each entry, check whether the package's `_test.go` files import `go.uber.org/goleak`. Absence → recommendation.

## Acceptance

A regenerated audit for a Go repo with goroutine-spawning packages emits a goleak recommendation listing each package and the suggested `TestMain` snippet.

## Related

Split out from `bento-638` (`go test -race`).

title: generate-audit: add Java analyzers (spotbugs, error-prone, OWASP dependency-check)
description:
## Gap

`catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md` and `audit-discover.py` have no Java entries. A repo with Java code (Maven or Gradle) gets `Java` in `project_shape.languages` but `missing_by_language["Java"]` is absent — the model has nothing to recommend.

Real audit (refute, 2026-05-02) has `adapters/openrewrite/` — Maven, Java 17, OpenRewrite 8.33.3, Jackson 2.17. The audit skill emitted nothing for Java; I had to write the recommendation by hand in the audit report.

## Why it matters

Java has a mature analyzer ecosystem. Omitting it from the skill means every Java-touching audit has to be hand-rolled.

## Suggested fix

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md`, add:

1. `### spotbugs` — bytecode analyzer. Maven: `mvn com.github.spotbugs:spotbugs-maven-plugin:check`. Gradle: `./gradlew spotbugsMain`. Severity: HIGH → `error`, MEDIUM → `warning`.
2. `### error-prone` — Google's compile-time checker. Maven plugin recommendation. Treat as compile-step augmentation rather than a separate run.
3. `### dependency-check` (OWASP) — Maven: `mvn org.owasp:dependency-check-maven:check`. Any CVE → `error`.
4. `### checkstyle` and `### spotless` — formatting/style; lower priority but worth an entry.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/scripts/audit-discover.py`:

5. Add Java entries to `_TOOL_PROBES` and `missing_by_language["Java"]` defaults.
6. Detect `pom.xml` vs `build.gradle` and emit the appropriate Maven or Gradle command in run blocks.

## Acceptance

A regenerated audit for a Java/Maven repo emits SpotBugs + OWASP dependency-check recommendations with the correct `mvn` invocation.

title: generate-audit: recommend Go fuzzing for risk-surface byte/string functions
description:
## Gap

`catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md` does not mention native Go fuzzing (`go test -fuzz`). The discovery script does not detect existing `Fuzz*` test functions or report fuzzing as a Go capability.

Real audit (refute, 2026-05-02) bug https://github.com/shatterproof-ai/refute/issues/7 — `transport.Read` allocates `make([]byte, contentLength)` from an untrusted header. A fuzz target on `transport.Read` would have surfaced the unbounded allocation immediately. Same for `parseWorkspaceEdit`, `positionToOffset`, `applyEdits` — pure functions with byte/string input, ideal fuzz targets.

## Why it matters

Native Go fuzzing has been stable since Go 1.18. It costs nothing to add when risk-surface functions already accept opaque bytes from external sources (parsers, decoders, framing, position math). The existing audit recommends coverage thresholds but ignores fuzzing entirely.

## Suggested fix

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md`:

1. Add `### go-fuzz` (or `### go test -fuzz`) entry. Run pattern: `go test -fuzz=FuzzX -fuzztime=30s ./pkg`. Interpretation: any new corpus entry produced under `testdata/fuzz/` during a CI run is a `warning`; any panic or unrecovered crash is `error`.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/discovery-checklist.md`:

2. Add a "Fuzz-target candidacy" surface. Flag any Go function whose signature is `func(...) ([]byte, error)`, `func([]byte) (T, error)`, or accepts a `string` parsed externally — these are the natural fuzz targets.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/scripts/audit-discover.py`:

3. Detect existing `Fuzz*` test functions via `git ls-files '*_test.go' | xargs grep -l 'func Fuzz'` and report count. Zero in a Go repo with parser/transport/decoder code → recommendation gap.

## Acceptance

A regenerated refute audit lists `transport.Read`, `parseWorkspaceEdit`, `positionToOffset`, and `applyEdits` as fuzz-target candidates with a worked `go test -fuzz=FuzzRead -fuzztime=30s ./internal/backend/lsp` example.

title: generate-audit: add semgrep as a cross-language audit module
description:
## Gap

`catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md` and `audit-discover.py` have no entry for semgrep. The audit's "static analysis" phase is per-language, so multi-language repos get N independent linter passes with zero cross-cutting pattern detection.

Real audit (refute, 2026-05-02) is a Go + Java + TypeScript repo. The current skill emits three separate (and three separately-broken) static-analysis lanes. None of them share a rule for "command injection," "path traversal," or "unsafe deserialization" across languages.

## Why it matters

`semgrep --config=auto .` runs language-agnostic AST-based pattern matching on Go, Java, JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Ruby, and more in a single pass. It is the single highest-leverage tool for multi-language repos and the audit skill ignores it.

## Suggested fix

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md`:

1. Add `### semgrep` entry. Run: `semgrep --config=auto --error --quiet .`. Interpretation: any `ERROR`-severity rule → audit `error`; `WARNING`-severity → audit `warning`. Treat the default registry as a starting point; encourage repo-specific rules under `.semgrep/`.
2. List semgrep under cross-language tools alongside gitleaks/trufflehog.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/scripts/audit-discover.py`:

3. Add semgrep to the cross-language tool list so it appears in `missing_cross_language` when not detected.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/SKILL.md`:

4. In the Audit Modules list, fold semgrep results under both "static analysis" and "security review" — its rules span both.

## Acceptance

A regenerated audit for any multi-language repo emits a `semgrep` recommendation in the static-analysis gaps section, with a worked command and interpretation block.

title: generate-audit: expand golangci-lint guidance to enable errorlint, wrapcheck, nilaway, exhaustive
description:
## Gap

`catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md` documents `golangci-lint` but says only "Surface all error-level findings as audit errors." It does not prescribe which linters within golangci-lint to enable. A repo that adopts golangci-lint with the default linter set gets `errcheck`, `gosimple`, `govet`, `ineffassign`, `staticcheck`, `unused` — useful, but missing the highest-leverage extras for Go codebases.

Real audit (refute, 2026-05-02): heavy use of `fmt.Errorf("...: %w", err)` (bug https://github.com/shatterproof-ai/refute/issues/5 surfaces error wrapping); `SymbolKind` enum and LSP `kinds` switches that should be exhaustive; nil-pointer paths through LSP JSON-RPC. None of these are caught by the default golangci-lint set.

## Why it matters

Configuring golangci-lint correctly is most of the value of golangci-lint. The skill that recommends installing it should also tell the model what to enable.

## Suggested fix

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md` golangci-lint entry:

1. Recommend an explicit baseline `.golangci.yml` enabling, in addition to the defaults: `errorlint`, `wrapcheck`, `exhaustive`, `nilaway` (Uber, optional but high signal), `prealloc`, `gocritic`, `revive`, `misspell`. Note that `errcheck` should remain enabled and not be silenced via `// nolint`.
2. Treat a `.golangci.yml` that *disables* any of `errcheck`, `staticcheck`, `unused`, `govet` as a finding-grade misconfiguration.
3. Treat absence of `errorlint` and `wrapcheck` in any Go repo with > 20 `fmt.Errorf("...%w", err)` call sites as a `warning`-level recommendation gap.

## Acceptance

A regenerated audit for a Go repo emits a recommended `.golangci.yml` snippet rather than the bare "install golangci-lint" line, and flags any existing config that disables core linters.

title: generate-audit: add docs-quality module (markdownlint, lychee, typos)
description:
## Gap

`catalog/skills/generate-audit/SKILL.md` lists "documentation coverage", "docs truthfulness", and "documentation utility" as audit modules. None of these include automated tooling — they are model-only passes. Yet there are robust automated checks for the docs-quality dimension that the skill never names.

Real audit (refute, 2026-05-02): `docs/` has 18+ markdown files, multiple internal links, typos likely in long-form spec docs, and a `docs/support-matrix.md` with an external link to GitHub issue #1 that the audit had to verify by hand. The skill emitted no doc-tooling recommendation.

## Why it matters

Three small, fast, language-agnostic tools cover most automated docs hygiene:

- `markdownlint-cli2` — markdown formatting consistency.
- `lychee` (or `markdown-link-check`) — broken internal/external links.
- `typos` (Rust binary) — typo detection that ignores code identifiers.

Together they take seconds to run, have near-zero false positives, and catch a class of doc-rot the model-based passes routinely miss.

## Suggested fix

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md`, add three entries: `markdownlint`, `lychee`, `typos`. Severity: any error → `warning`; broken external link → `error`.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/scripts/audit-discover.py`:

1. Add the three tools to `missing_cross_language` default set when the repo has `*.md` files.
2. Surface them in `documentation_analysis` results.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/SKILL.md` Audit Modules:

3. Promote a "documentation hygiene (automated)" module that runs the three tools, distinct from the model-based "documentation utility" pass.

## Acceptance

A regenerated audit for any repo with markdown docs emits a docs-hygiene phase with markdownlint, lychee, and typos commands plus interpretation severity.

title: generate-audit: add 'go test -race' to Phase 1 build health for Go repos with concurrency
description:
## Gap

`catalog/skills/generate-audit/` does not recommend `go test -race`. The Go race detector is the highest-yield Go-native check the skill currently omits.

Real audit (refute, 2026-05-02): codebase has `progressTracker` (mutex + channels), `Client.pending` (mutex-guarded map mutated from `readLoop` and per-call `request`), `sync.Once` in `Shutdown`, atomic `nextID`. Adjacent to https://github.com/shatterproof-ai/refute/issues/8 — `-race` would force the goroutine interleavings that bug depends on. The audit didn't run `-race` because the skill never told it to.

## Why it matters

Single line to add. ~2× test wall-clock, ~5× memory. Catches races no static analyzer can. Should run on every Go test invocation in any repo with goroutines.

## Suggested fix

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/SKILL.md` and the per-language audit-phase scaffolding:

1. In Phase 1 (build health) for any repo where `concurrency_signals` is non-empty, run `go test -race -timeout 120s ./...` instead of (or alongside) `go test ./...`.
2. Treat any race finding as audit `error`.
3. Treat absence of `-race` in repo CI as a `warning`-level finding when the codebase has goroutines.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/scripts/audit-discover.py`:

4. Add a `concurrency_signals` field reporting presence of `go func`, `sync.Mutex`, `chan `, `sync.Once`, `sync/atomic` in non-test Go files. Empty signals → `-race` is overkill; non-empty → recommend.

## Acceptance

A regenerated audit for a Go repo with goroutines emits `go test -race` in the Phase 1 build-health step. The audit-discover JSON contains a `concurrency_signals` field listing matched files.

## Related

`goleak` was scoped together with `-race` in the original filing; split out as a separate issue (test-time goroutine-leak detector, different runtime mechanism, different config surface).

title: closure: ready-to-land log on merged branch is silently cleaned up
description: SKILL.md says: 'A ready-to-land log on a merged branch is an anomaly — land-work's cleanup pass did not run. Surface it to the user before any cleanup action.' But --apply delete-local-merged-branches deletes those branches silently: removable_merged_worktree_reason only blocks when checkpoint && checkpoint != 'ready-to-land'. Fix: emit a warnings entry per such branch ('ready-to-land log on merged branch — land-work cleanup did not run for <branch>') so the anomaly is visible without blocking the cleanup.

title: closure: detect squash/rebase-landed branches that still have worktrees
description: When tracker issue is CLOSED, the branch's diff is on primary under a different SHA (squash/rebase landing), and the linked worktree was never cleaned up, the branch falls into checked_out_in_worktree forever. Two helpers fail: merged_checked_out requires ancestor reachability (different SHA breaks it); patch_equivalent_review excludes worktree-checked-out branches. User is forced to manual git worktree remove + git branch -D, which the skill's safety rules otherwise prohibit. Fix: classify patch-equivalent-with-worktree (e.g. landed_squash_with_worktree); detect via patch-id equivalence after stripping launch-work-log noise commits; expose apply mode (or fold into existing) that removes worktree + force-deletes branch.

title: SessionStart hook: auto-authorize bento worktree paths in additionalDirectories
description:
Reading or editing files in linked worktrees created by launch-work currently triggers a Claude Code permission prompt because the worktree path lives outside the project root. Plugins cannot ship permissions.additionalDirectories declaratively (only agent and subagentStatusLine keys are honored), so this needs to be done at runtime.

Add a SessionStart hook to the bento plugin that ensures the documented default worktree root (~/.local/share/worktrees) and the parent directories of any existing linked worktrees in the current repo are present in ~/.claude/settings.json under permissions.additionalDirectories. Idempotent, silent on no-op, non-fatal on failure (a malformed settings file must never break the user's session).

Caveat: settings.json edits affect future sessions, not the current one — the very first session after install still prompts. This is acceptable.

Out of scope: a machine-readable override declaration for repos that override the default worktree root. Repos using overrides will be self-healed on the session after their first worktree is created.
close_reason: Landed in main as merge commit a55c9349; SessionStart hook ensure-worktree-permissions.py auto-authorizes ~/.local/share/worktrees and observed linked-worktree parents in permissions.additionalDirectories

title: launch-work: move .launch-work/log.md out of the working tree
description:
Per ~/tmp/bento-closure-cleanup-problems.md and follow-up analysis: launch-work commits progress markers to the task branch. land-work-clean-log.py rebases them out before merging, but this is reactive: --keep-commits leaks commits, the cleanup itself adds a 'chore(launch-work-log): remove' commit, and any non-land-work merger leaks the whole sequence. Recent main shows leaks (b4dd194 ready-to-land, 183ce11 verification-passed, 55a2f18 remove).

Fix:
- Store the log at $GIT_DIR/launch-work/log.md (per linked worktree, this resolves to <primary>/.git/worktrees/<name>/launch-work/log.md). Anything under .git/ is structurally untrackable.
- Update launch-work-log.py (init/update/read) to write/read that path.
- Update launch-work-discover.py to enumerate worktrees via git and read each worktree's GIT_DIR/launch-work/log.md.
- Retire the rebase-and-drop dance in land-work-clean-log.py once the working-tree path is gone (or keep it as a one-shot migration aid).
- Add a land-work precondition that hard-fails if the candidate diff contains '^chore(launch-work-log):' commits or any '.launch-work/' paths — defense in depth.
- Document the invariant in launch-work and land-work SKILL.md: 'launch-work scaffolding never lands on the integration branch.'

Migration: existing branches still carry .launch-work/log.md; either leave them and let the precondition flag them at landing, or write a one-shot migration script.

Acceptance:
- launch-work-log.py writes under $GIT_DIR, never the working tree.
- A fresh launch-work + land-work cycle leaves zero new chore(launch-work-log) commits and zero .launch-work/ paths in the merge candidate.
- New land-work precondition test fails on a synthetic feature branch carrying chore(launch-work-log) commits.

title: land-work: preview worktrees leak under /tmp/land-work-preview-*
description:
land-work-create-preview.py calls git worktree add into a tempfile.mkdtemp directory under /tmp/land-work-preview-* but never removes it. Every landing leaves behind a registered git worktree. Currently 57 stale entries in /tmp.

Fix:
- Wrap the worktree creation in try/finally and run 'git worktree remove --force <preview_dir>' in the finally block.
- Verification consumers already work from the preview tree SHA, not the worktree path, so no caller needs the directory to persist.
- One-shot cleanup of the existing 57: enumerate via 'git worktree list --porcelain' and remove each.

Acceptance:
- land-work-create-preview.py removes its temp worktree before exit on both success and failure paths.
- New test exercises the success and failure paths, asserting 'git worktree list' does not retain the preview entry.
- Repo's existing /tmp/land-work-preview-* are cleaned up.

title: swarm: serialize human-gated handoffs through team lead
description:
Backgrounded swarm teammates cannot reach the user directly — the user typically only watches the team-lead window. When a teammate hits a blocking step that requires human attention (visual review, manual test approval, destructive-op confirmation, ambiguous-scope escalation), the current swarm skill has no documented pattern for routing that handoff through the lead.

Add an explicit pattern to the swarm skill:

1. Teammates that hit a human-gated step park their work in a safe state (branch unmerged, beads issue still open, worktree intact) and send a structured handoff message to the lead via SendMessage. The teammate then idles. It does NOT proceed to land-work or any destructive cleanup.

2. The lead is the single user-attention surface. When handoff messages arrive, the lead serializes them: surfaces ONE review request at a time (or a single batched message with explicit ordering when multiple are pending), in its own window where the user is committed to watching. Never let multiple teammates ping the user in parallel through different channels.

3. The user replies to the lead. The lead routes the decision (approve / revise / reject) back to the originating teammate via SendMessage. The teammate never reads the user directly.

4. The handoff message format should include: branch name, beads/tracker ID, what the teammate did (one-line summary), what the human needs to do (the exact command to run, e.g., 'open vizdiff' or 'run manual test X'), what to look for, and how to reply.

This is broader than visual review and applies to any human-gated step in any swarm-managed flow.

Related: bento-2xe (project-supplied hook contract). When a launch/land hook exits with the 'requires human handoff' code inside a swarm teammate, the teammate uses this pattern to escalate to the lead rather than just stopping silently.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Swarm skill documents a "human-gated handoff" section with the four rules above.
2. Handoff message format is specified concretely enough that two teammates produce comparable handoffs.
3. Lead's serialization behavior is documented (one at a time, or batched with ordering — never parallel pings).
4. Pattern is described generically; visual review is one example, not the only one.

title: launch-work and land-work: project-supplied hook extension points
description:
launch-work and land-work currently have no structured way for a project to inject pre-launch or pre-merge work (visual baselines, DB snapshots, cache warm-up, manual review gates). Today this is encoded as prose in CLAUDE.md and depends on the agent reading and honoring it.

Add a generic hook contract:

- Well-known directory or config-listed entry where projects ship executable hook scripts.
- launch-work runs project-supplied launch hooks before any edits. Non-zero exit aborts launch.
- land-work runs project-supplied land hooks before merge. Non-zero exit aborts merge.
- Define a documented "requires human handoff" exit code (e.g., exit 75) that both skills recognize. When a hook exits with that code, the skill stops, surfaces a handoff message containing the hook's stdout, and does NOT abort destructively — the work parks safely with the branch+worktree intact for a human to resolve.
- Hooks receive standard env vars: branch name, base ref SHA, head SHA, repo root, runtime (claude/codex), task ID if available.

This must be designed so projects without any hooks behave exactly as today. No bento-side dependency on any specific tool (bugshot, vizdiff, etc.) — the hook directory is opaque to bento.

Related downstream consumer: bugshot will be a typical hook author for projects that want visual-review gates, but bento must not name bugshot anywhere. A project using a different visual-diff tool, or no visual review at all, must work identically.
acceptance_criteria:
1. launch-work skill documents a project-hook discovery and execution contract.
2. land-work skill documents the same, plus the 'requires human handoff' exit code semantics.
3. A reference example shows a project shipping a no-op hook and a hook that returns the human-handoff exit code.
4. Skills work unchanged for projects that ship no hooks.
5. No mention of any specific tool (bugshot, vizdiff, playwright) anywhere in the bento skills.
