title: beads-issue-flow: prevent close without verified land
description:
## Problem

A beads issue can be closed (`bd update <id> --status closed` or `bd close`) even when its branch was never merged to the integration branch. This silently breaks dependents: downstream tasks that `depends-on` the closed issue believe their prerequisite is on main, so they get scheduled / claimed / worked, then fail at first reference to the missing code.

## How it happened (real example, 2026-04-29 in kapow)

kapow-pwkn.5 (Fit state machine, exports `fit.Step`) was marked closed in beads but its branch `kapow-pwkn-5-fit-state-machine` never landed on main. Worse, the branch had drifted off an ancient main and showed -53k lines vs current main, so a naive merge would erase huge swaths of recent work.

Dependent kapow-pwkn.6.4 (`fitNextTurn` resolver, calls `fit.Step`) was marked ready by beads. A swarm picked it up, spawned a teammate, who hit the missing symbol on first compile. We had to:
1. Reopen pwkn.5
2. Cherry-pick its 3 implementation commits onto a fresh branch off current main
3. Re-land
4. Then resume pwkn.6.4

Pure recovery work that wouldn't have existed if beads had refused to close pwkn.5 without a verified land.

## Suggested fixes (any of)

1. **`bd close` requires evidence of land.** Accept `--landed-commit <sha>` and verify the commit is reachable from the configured integration branch ref. Without the flag, refuse the close (or warn loudly and require `--force`).
2. **`beads-issue-flow` skill enforces this at the skill layer.** Skill never closes an issue unless it just successfully ran the project's land-work flow and captured the merge SHA. Today's skill apparently allows close before/independent of land.
3. **Periodic doctor command.** `bd doctor` (or similar) walks closed issues, checks their feature branch (if known) was actually merged, flags stragglers. Less safe than prevention but catches historical bugs.

Option 1 is cleanest if the close-time landing SHA can be captured reliably. Option 2 is cheaper to ship and catches the same pattern in practice since most closes flow through the skill.

## Acceptance

- A closed beads issue's branch must be reachable from the integration branch (or the close was explicitly forced with a documented reason).
- Demonstrate by attempting to repeat the kapow-pwkn.5 mistake against a test issue: the close attempt fails (or warns and requires --force).
- Document the new behavior in the beads-issue-flow skill so swarm and other consumers stop assuming closed = landed without a check.

title: closure: add --correlate-branches flag for review_required triage with tracker correlation (beads/gh/jira)
description:
## Problem

`closure-scan.py` classifies unmerged-not-in-worktree branches as `review_required` — a dumping ground requiring per-branch manual investigation (cherry, log --grep, tracker lookup) to decide keep vs delete. Observed concretely on kapow 2026-04-29: 7 such branches, all 500-700 commits ahead of main, names embedding closed beads issues.

The signals are deterministic; only the verdict needs human judgment.

## Solution

Add opt-in `--correlate-branches` flag to `catalog/skills/closure/scripts/closure-scan.py` that emits a `correlation` block on each `review_required` branch entry. Raw signals only — no verdict, no new apply mode.

### Signals (per branch)

- `issue_id` — extracted from branch name via tracker-confirmed lookup
- `cherry_unique_count`, `cherry_equivalent_count` — from `git cherry main <branch>`
- `main_commits_referencing_issue` — `git log main --grep=<id> --since=<merge-base-date>` (bounded)
- `tracker_status` — from tracker shim
- `merge_base_age_days`, `divergence_ahead`, `divergence_behind`

`files_touched_*` deferred — heuristic value unclear; cherry+grep+tracker triple should be sufficient.

### Tracker shim

Auto-detect with `--tracker {beads,gh,jira,none}` override:

- `.beads/` present → beads (`bd show <id> --json`, parse `status`)
- `.github/` + `gh` available → gh (`gh issue view <id> --json state`, parse `state`)
- JIRA configured (env: `JIRA_BASE_URL` + `JIRA_API_TOKEN` + `JIRA_USER_EMAIL`, or `.jira/config`) → jira (`curl` to `/rest/api/3/issue/<KEY>?fields=status`, parse `fields.status.name`)
- else → none, `tracker_status: null`

JIRA issue IDs are `[A-Z]+-[0-9]+` (e.g. `PROJ-123`); regex differs from beads/gh.

### Issue-ID extraction

CLI flag `--issue-pattern <regex>` overrides default. Default derived from detected tracker:

- beads: shape from `bd list --json` (typically `[a-z]+-[a-z0-9]+`)
- gh: numeric `#?(\d+)` or repo-prefix convention
- jira: `([A-Z]+-[0-9]+)`

Only populate `issue_id` if tracker recognizes the candidate (lookup confirms existence). No new repo-local config file.

### Scope rules

- Emit `correlation` block only when classification is `review_required` (other classifications already encode disposition).
- Off by default; opt-in because `git cherry` is slow.
- Skip cleanly when no tracker is configured.

### SKILL.md updates

Add decision matrix as agent-facing guidance. Matrix MUST include explicit per-branch user-confirmation gate — heuristic deletion is never automated.

| cherry | tracker | grep on main | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|---|
| all - | closed | hits | Landed under different SHA. Recommend delete. |
| all - | open | hits | Patches landed but tracker still open. Delete branch + hand off to tracker skill to close. |
| all - | (any) | hits | Landed under different SHA. Recommend delete. |
| mostly + | closed | hits | Issue done but this branch's code never merged — likely abandoned re-implementation. Spot-check then delete. |
| mostly + | open | none | Genuine outstanding work. Keep. |
| mostly + | open | hits | Partial overlap. Human review. |
| mostly + | (no issue) | none | Old branch, no tracker context. Human decision. |

## Out of scope

- Auto-applied cleanup based on correlation signals (no new `--apply` mode)
- Tracker mutation (still owned by beads-issue-flow / github-issue-flow / future jira-issue-flow)
- Branch-name pattern auto-discovery beyond tracker-confirmed lookup
- `files_touched_*` signal (deferred)
- Repo-local config file (`bento.toml` / `.bento/`) — use CLI flags instead

## References

Spec: /tmp/bento-issue-correlation.md (this session)

title: beads-issue-flow skill: document closure command (bd close --reason vs bd update --notes)
description:
Agents writing implementation plans for Beads-tracked repos frequently emit `bd update <id> --status closed --reason done -m "..."` to close issues. None of `--reason`, `-m`, or `--message` exist on `bd update` in the current Beads build, so the closure step fails the first time it runs and the agent has to scramble to find the right form. This wastes review iterations.

## Root cause

There are two valid closure paths in `bd` and they take **different flags**:

- `bd close <id> --reason "..."` (or alias: `bd done`) — `--reason`/`-r` is the supported flag here.
- `bd update <id> --status closed --notes "..."` — `--notes` is the only message-bearing flag on `update`. `--reason` does not exist here.

The kapow design-system rollout (kapow-hrj4) ran into this five times in a row across foundation tasks 1–5 because the plan template prescribed the wrong shape.

## Skill gap

`catalog/skills/beads-issue-flow/SKILL.md` should:

1. Recommend `bd close <id> --reason "..."` as the canonical closure form. It's purpose-built for this and the `--reason` flag matches mental model.
2. Note that `bd update --status closed` is a fine fallback when you also need to set other fields atomically (e.g., `--add-label`, `--assignee`), but you must use `--notes` (not `--reason`/`-m`) for the closure message.
3. Mention the related pair: `bd note <id> "..."` is shorthand for `bd update <id> --append-notes "..."` — agents reach for `-m` here too.

Sister concern (out of scope; track separately if needed): `bd update` accepts `--description` to replace the body but agents often want to *append*; the skill could call out `--append-notes` and the absence of `--append-description`.

## Acceptance

`catalog/skills/beads-issue-flow/SKILL.md` Common Commands section gains an explicit Closure subsection covering the two forms above, with the right flag on each. Sibling skill bento-blk already touches dependency-link guidance; that's a good adjacent reference.

Verified against `bd update --help` and `bd close --help` output 2026-04-28.

title: beads-issue-flow skill: add dependency-link guidance to prevent inverted edges
description:
Agents filing Beads issues frequently invert blocker/blocked direction when creating dependency links.

## Root cause

The Beads CLI exposes two ways to create a blocks edge with **opposite** argument orders:

- `bd link A B` (default --type blocks) → B blocks A
- `bd dep add A B` → B blocks A (same as link)
- `bd dep A --blocks B` → A blocks B (opposite!)

The positional forms read unnaturally in English (subject-last: 'bd dep add <blocked> <blocker>'), so agents pattern-match wrong about half the time.

## Skill gap

`catalog/skills/beads-issue-flow/SKILL.md` lists Common Commands but omits `bd link` / `bd dep` entirely. There is no guidance on:

- Which form to prefer
- Which argument is blocker vs blocked
- How to verify a link after creation

## Proposed fix

Add a 'Dependency Links' section after 'Common Commands':

1. Canonical form: `bd dep <blocker> --blocks <blocked>` — flag name disambiguates direction at the call site. Forbid positional `bd link` / `bd dep add` in agent-authored commands.
2. Mandatory verification: after creating, run `bd dep list <blocked>` and confirm the blocker appears under 'blocked by', not 'blocks'.
3. One worked example: English statement → command → expected verification output.

Behavioral skill change → version bump + scripts/build-plugins required per AGENTS.md.

title: Resolve the TodoWrite instruction conflict between bento CLAUDE.md and superpowers skills
description:
bento CLAUDE.md prohibits TodoWrite/TaskCreate ("do NOT use TodoWrite... use bd for ALL task tracking") while installed superpowers skills mandate it (e.g. executing-plans: "Create TodoWrite and proceed"; using-superpowers' checklist flow). Any plan execution in bento puts the agent in direct instruction conflict. Options: (a) add a one-line carve-out ("when a third-party skill requires TodoWrite for ephemeral in-session checklists, allow it; durable tracking stays in bd"), or (b) disable the conflicting superpowers skills for this project. Precedent: the dotfiles `documentation-paths.md` rule was created to patch a different superpowers behavior. Decide and implement; keep wording within the conciseness budget.

Acceptance: an agent executing a plan in bento has a single consistent instruction; the chosen rule documented in exactly one place.

Post-review note: this is an operator policy decision — filed for triage and flagged for human decision. Unresolved question: carve-out (a) vs disabling the conflicting superpowers skills (b).

title: Lint temporal claims ("follow-up", "for now", "not yet") in catalog markdown unless annotated with a tracker ID
description:
bentobug's step 5 promised a follow-up that shipped a month earlier without the prose updating — the claim class is greppable. Work: add a lint over `catalog/skills/**/*.md` flagging "follow-up", "not yet", "coming soon", "for now", "will be added" unless the same line (or an adjacent annotation) carries a tracker issue ID (e.g. `bento-xxx`); run in the standard gate. The companion prose rule (beads-issue-flow close step greps for the issue ID) closes the loop when the referenced issue ships. Expect a small allowlist for legitimate uses (e.g. instructions telling the agent to say "for now").

Acceptance: lint fails on the pre-fix bentobug step 5 text (regression fixture); current corpus passes after fixes or annotations.

title: Repo hygiene sweep: stale committed log, __pycache__ gitignore, ERRORS.md stub, untracked artifacts
description:
Small items in one sweep: (1) `.launch-work/log.md` at repo root is a stale committed template from the removed progress-log feature (checkpoint "ready-to-land", last-updated 2026-04-30) — remove and gitignore `.launch-work/`. (2) `__pycache__/` dirs sit untracked in five catalog scripts dirs, one containing a stale .pyc from a renamed module — add a gitignore entry. (3) `ERRORS.md` is a 9-byte stub (`# Errors`), intentionally seeded for the project-memory skill's durable error log but indistinguishable from cruft — add one explanatory line under the heading. (4) `.worktrees/` at repo root is empty, ignored only via the user's global gitignore, and contradicts the documented `~/.local/share/worktrees/` policy — remove it and/or add a repo-local ignore. (5) `.beads/issues.jsonl` shows staged-but-uncommitted in git status — verify the beads sync hooks have it consistent and commit.

Acceptance: clean `git status` on main; gitignore covers .launch-work/ and __pycache__/; ERRORS.md self-describes.

title: code-bloat-sniffer: remove hard dependency on superpowers plugin
description:
`catalog/skills/code-bloat-sniffer/SKILL.md` step 4 makes `superpowers:dispatching-parallel-agents` mandatory ("this is the default, not a fallback") — a cross-marketplace dependency; on installs without superpowers the workflow's central step is unfollowable. Add a one-line fallback: "if superpowers is not installed, dispatch parallel Task subagents directly with one chunk per subagent." Rebuild/bump.

Acceptance: the skill is executable without superpowers installed; the superpowers path remains preferred when present.

title: Trim ~1,500 words from the largest skills: boundary-rule repetition, hook boilerplate, model-guidance duplication, stack-skill genericism
description:
Targeted cuts, no behavior loss: (1) land-work/closure restate their mutual cleanup boundary ~10 times across descriptions, sections, anti-rationalization rows, and helper-output.md — state it once per skill plus one anti-rationalization row (closure's internal dedup is a separate filed issue). (2) launch-work and land-work each contain four near-identical `run-lifecycle-extensions.py` invocation blocks (~100 lines combined) — one parameterized block plus the timing table in project-hook-scripts.md. (3) Every skill with `recommended_model:` frontmatter restates it in a prose "Model Guidance" section — keep only escalation criteria (one line); backfill the 8 skills missing the frontmatter field (incl. dev-skill); update or drop DESIGN.md's half-coverage model table (~lines 184-199). (4) launch-work states the tracker-only exclusion three times; issue-readiness-check repeats "don't skip the fresh reviewer" three times — once each. (5) Stack skills (go-pgx-goose, react-vite-mantine, graphql-gqlgen-gql-tada) are ~half baseline senior-engineer knowledge; keep discovery-first ordering, repo-conventions-over-baseline, fail-first tests, and the gql.tada sync workflow; cut generic best practices (~30-40% each).

Acceptance: aggregate catalog SKILL.md word count drops at least 1,200 words; per-skill behavior checklists unchanged (diff review); rebuild/bump.

title: De-duplicate README/DESIGN/AGENTS: one home per convention, cross-links elsewhere
description:
The same conventions are restated across files and have already rotted in sync (the plugins-layout error existed in README and DESIGN simultaneously): worktree convention (README ~47-81 vs DESIGN ~85-105), agent-plugins convention (README ~83-93, DESIGN ~107-136, AGENTS.md), plugin format (README ~240-295 vs DESIGN ~56-83), extension points (README ~307-322, AGENTS.md, docs/extensions.md). Target ownership: DESIGN.md = decisions and rationale only; README = operational reference; AGENTS.md = agent rules; each topic stated once with cross-links. Also fix bento/CLAUDE.md line 1 ("a Claude and Codex plugin", singular) to marketplace framing per AGENTS.md's own rule.

Depends on: refresh-install-and-layout-docs (do not dedupe stale text). Acceptance: each convention has exactly one normative home; other mentions are one-line links; no contradiction remains between the three files.

Acceptance (amended): produce a per-topic ownership checklist (worktree convention, agent-plugins convention, plugin format, extension points, beads guidance); for each topic, exactly one normative section remains and all other mentions are one-line links — verify by grepping each topic's heading keywords and listing every occurrence in the PR description.

title: Port beads-issue-flow's mechanical closure-evidence check to github-issue-flow
description:
beads-issue-flow's Closure Evidence Rule (SKILL.md ~lines 120-147) requires `git merge-base --is-ancestor <merge-sha> <integration-branch>` before closing — mechanical and testable. github-issue-flow (~lines 30-32) has only prose ("after the verified landing"). The failure mode — closing unlanded work, breaking dependents — is tracker-independent. Work: port the ancestry check (~6 lines) into github-issue-flow's close step, keeping wording consistent with the beads sibling. Rebuild/bump.

Acceptance: github-issue-flow contains the same mechanical pre-close check; wording cross-checked against beads-issue-flow.

title: Add lifecycle status headers to docs/specs and docs/plans; backfill; lint for presence
description:
All 25 spec/plan files are date-prefixed but only 3 carry any `Status:` field, and none is marked implemented or superseded. Concrete confusion: `docs/specs/2026-04-26-launch-work-progress-log-design.md` specifies a feature wholly removed by commit 757495a and reads as current; `docs/specs/2026-04-22-expedition-swarm-design.md` describes an integration never built as designed; three shipped features have plan files showing 0/27-46 checkboxes done; the agent-plugins convention spec says `Status: Draft` while DESIGN.md calls it ratified. Also: four `*-plan.md` files live in `docs/specs/` while eight live in `docs/plans/` with no stated rule.

Work: define a small status vocabulary (`draft|approved|implemented|superseded-by:<file>|abandoned`); backfill all 25 files (the named ones above have known ground truth; for the rest, a quick per-spec code check); flip agent-plugins to ratified; move or document the plan-file split; add a lint asserting every file under docs/specs and docs/plans has a status header.

Acceptance: every spec/plan carries a status; the named stale specs are marked superseded/implemented; lint enforces presence going forward.

title: State launch-work vs expedition precedence; align launch-work's description with its actual scope
description:
(1) launch-work's frontmatter says "Hard trigger — always invoke before any code or file edits," but inside an expedition, task branches must be cut from the expedition base via `expedition.py start-task`, and launch-work's bootstrap defaults to the detected primary branch. Neither skill states which wins; handoff handles its expedition overlap explicitly ("When NOT to use: inside an active expedition") and is the model to copy — add the carve-out to launch-work and/or a precedence line to expedition. (2) launch-work's description says "Creates branch+worktree" but steps 11-13 govern TDD discipline, post-hooks, and ready-to-land summaries — the whole lifecycle up to the landing boundary. Either trim those steps or make the description honest so agents know reloading it late in a task is meaningful.

Acceptance: an agent inside an expedition reading both skills has an unambiguous instruction; launch-work's description matches its content; rebuilt/bumped.

title: Add a check that documented CLI invocations satisfy their scripts' required arguments
description:
expedition's SKILL.md documented `close-task` without the parser-required `--summary` (fixed separately); nothing prevents the same class elsewhere — every skill helper is a subprocess CLI whose usage is documented in prose. Work: add a parity check that, for each documented usage line in catalog SKILL.md/reference files, locates the named script and subcommand, introspects its argparse parser, and asserts every `required=True` flag appears in the documented line. Pragmatic v1: a small per-skill manifest mapping documented command lines to (script path, subcommand) so the check does not parse arbitrary prose. Run in the standard gate.

Acceptance: the check fails on a regression fixture reproducing the expedition `--summary` omission; passes on the current corpus after fixes.

title: Document PyInstaller as Python dependency packaging option
description:
# Document PyInstaller as Python dependency packaging option

Update project documentation to mention PyInstaller as a potential method for including dependencies in Bento Python scripts. Acceptance: relevant project documentation names PyInstaller alongside other dependency-inclusion approaches without changing generated plugin artifacts. In scope: docs-only wording. Out of scope: implementation changes, generated plugin rebuilds, or version bump unless the edited docs require it.

title: generate-audit: recommend SBOM generation only for repos that ship pre-built binaries or containers
description:
## Gap

`catalog/skills/generate-audit/` has nothing on SBOMs. Tools: `cyclonedx-gomod` (Go), `syft` (cross-language, containers).

## When SBOMs matter

The payoff is downstream:

- **Vuln scanning at scale**: `osv-scanner` reads SBOMs and is faster/more reliable for shipped binaries than re-deriving the dep graph each run.
- **Supply-chain attestation**: SLSA Level 2+ and enterprise procurement increasingly require SBOMs.
- **License compliance for shipped artifacts**: covered by `go-licenses` for source consumers, but binary consumers can't run `go-licenses`.

For source-only distribution (`go install` from public repo), an SBOM is mostly redundant — consumers rebuild the graph from `go.mod`.

## Why P3

Universal SBOM recommendation is noise. The honest gate is **behavioral**: does the repo ship pre-built artifacts that consumers can't rebuild from source?

## Suggested fix — gate on shipment shape

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/scripts/audit-discover.py`:

1. Detect shipment artifacts:
   - `.goreleaser.yml` / `.goreleaser.yaml` (Go binary releases)
   - `Dockerfile` / `Containerfile` / `oci-image-build.yml`
   - GitHub Releases via `gh api repos/<owner>/<repo>/releases?per_page=1` (when `gh` is available)
   - Helm chart, Debian / RPM packaging, Homebrew formula
2. Emit `ships_binaries: bool` and `ships_containers: bool` in discovery JSON.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md`:

3. Add `### cyclonedx-gomod` and `### syft` entries. Mark both as conditional: only recommend when `ships_binaries` or `ships_containers` is true.
4. Severity: absence in a binary-shipping repo → `warning`. Absence in a source-only repo → no finding.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/SKILL.md`:

5. Add a "supply chain" audit module that fires only when shipment is detected. Module covers SBOM, signing/attestation (cosign, SLSA), and reproducible builds.

## Refute-specific note

Refute ships only via `go install` from source. SBOM is premature. Revisit when there's a release pipeline producing pre-built binaries (e.g. when goreleaser lands).

## Acceptance

A regenerated audit for a Go repo with `.goreleaser.yml` recommends `cyclonedx-gomod`. A regenerated audit for a source-only Go repo (like refute today) does not.
close_reason: Dropped on review: SBOM recommendation is too narrow for the audit skill. The behavioral gate (ships pre-built binaries / containers) excludes most repos correctly, but for the small set that does ship artifacts, the maintainers already know they need an SBOM — they're driven by enterprise procurement or SLSA compliance, not by an audit nudge. The audit skill adds little value here. Closing without implementation.

title: generate-audit: detect library-shaped Go repos and require Example* functions for public types
description:
## Gap

`catalog/skills/generate-audit/` has audit modules for "documentation coverage", "docs truthfulness", and "documentation utility" — all model-based. None of them check whether a Go package's public API has runnable `Example*` functions, which is the Go-native way to make docs and tests the same artifact.

A `func ExampleFoo()` (or `ExampleType_method`) does three things at once:

1. Renders as a code sample under the symbol on pkg.go.dev / `go doc`.
2. Compiles against current code, so it can't drift past breaking API changes.
3. Runs as a test with output verification via `// Output:` comments.

Audits that only check doc *presence* miss the case where a repo has plenty of prose docs that no longer match the code.

## Why P3 (not P2)

Examples-as-tests pay off on **library-shaped** repos with public packages. Binaries / `main`-only / `internal`-only repos get nothing — Go doesn't render examples for unexported packages. Universal recommendation makes the skill noisy.

The audit should detect library shape (public packages with non-trivial public API) and emit the recommendation conditionally.

## Suggested fix

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/scripts/audit-discover.py`:

1. Detect library shape: count Go files outside `internal/`, `cmd/`, `_test.go` with at least one exported (capitalized) symbol. Emit `library_shape_packages` listing them.
2. For each, check existing `Example*` test functions. Per-package coverage = (packages with at least one example) / (library-shape packages).

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/SKILL.md`:

3. Add a sub-module under documentation utility: "Example coverage." For library-shape packages with no `Example*` functions, emit a `warning`-level finding listing the package and its primary public symbols. Do not fire on `internal/` or `main` packages.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md`:

4. Add `### Example* functions` reference. Not a tool — a Go-native pattern. Note that `// Output:` comments make the example a regression test; encourage their use.
5. Cross-cutting: where the audit also detects Go code blocks in `README.md` or other docs, flag them as "unverified" if the corresponding package has no `Example*`. README code that doesn't compile is a stale-doc bug.

## Acceptance

A regenerated audit for a library-shaped Go repo (e.g. one whose top-level packages export public API) emits a per-package example-coverage finding. A regenerated audit for a CLI-only or internal-only repo (like refute today) does not emit the finding.

## Refute-specific note

Refute is currently CLI-only — the issue does not apply. Revisit when `internal/edit` types or any `refute` package graduate to a public library API.
close_reason: Dropped on review: Example* idiom is too narrow to recommend at audit-skill level. Heavily used in Go stdlib and a few prominent public libraries (cobra, gorilla, x/*), rarely elsewhere. The // Output: comment requirement makes the pattern awkward for any example that doesn't naturally print, and the discovery payoff (pkg.go.dev rendering) only fires for published public libraries. The honest gate would exclude almost every repo we'd actually audit. Closing without implementation.

title: generate-audit: add architecture/import-graph rule module gated on package count and churn
description:
## Gap

`catalog/skills/generate-audit/` does not surface architecture-rule tools. A Go repo with implicit layering (presentation → domain → leaf) gets no recommendation to *encode* that layering, even when discovery sees a deep `internal/` tree.

Tools: `go-arch-lint` (Go-native, declarative YAML), `import-boss` (Kubernetes-style, package-scoped). Both encode allowed and forbidden import edges.

## Why it matters

Real audit (refute, 2026-05-02). Layering exists implicitly:

- `cmd/refute` → `internal/cli` → `internal/backend` → `internal/edit` / `internal/symbol`
- `internal/cli` should reach `internal/backend/lsp` only via `internal/backend.Backend` interface
- `internal/edit` is leaf; no upward imports

The audit had no module for "is this layering enforced?" The recommendation came from manual review of import statements.

## Why P3 (not P2)

Architecture rules pay off on **drift-prone** codebases: many packages, many contributors, active churn. On small repos with obvious layering, the rules are ceremony — the structure survives because every contributor can hold it in their head. Unconditional recommendation makes the skill noisy.

## Suggested fix — sizing gate

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/scripts/audit-discover.py`:

1. Count Go packages, count distinct git authors over last 12 months in non-test files, count import-graph edges.
2. Emit `architecture_rules_recommended` boolean: true when `packages > 8` AND `authors > 2` AND no `.go-arch-lint.yml` / `import-boss` config detected.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md`:

3. Add `### go-arch-lint` entry. Run: `go-arch-lint check --project-path .`. Severity: violation → `error`.
4. Note `import-boss` as alternative for Kubernetes-style per-package rules.
5. Treat absence of architecture rules in a sized-up repo (per gate above) as a `warning`-level recommendation gap. Below the gate, do not emit the recommendation.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/SKILL.md`:

6. Add audit module "architecture rules" alongside contract/schema consistency. Output: discovered layering as a graph, recommended `.go-arch-lint.yml` skeleton, drift findings.

## Refute-specific note for the audit author

Today refute does not need go-arch-lint (small, single-author, clear layering). Revisit when the package count exceeds ~8 internal packages or when a second contributor lands a non-trivial PR.

## Acceptance

A regenerated audit for a small Go repo with clear layering does *not* recommend go-arch-lint. A regenerated audit for a large multi-author Go repo with no architecture config *does* recommend it, with a generated skeleton config based on detected layering.
close_reason: Dropped on review: architecture/import-graph rules are narrowly applicable (gated on package count + author count). Even with a sizing gate, the recommendation only fires for a small slice of repos. Per the broadly-applicable rule, dropping. Repos that need this tend to know they need it.

title: generate-audit: add mutation testing as a coverage-quality module gated on existing line coverage
description:
## Gap

`catalog/skills/generate-audit/` recommends coverage tools but treats coverage as a quality signal in itself. It does not distinguish "code was executed" from "code was tested." Mutation testing is the standard tool for that distinction and the skill omits it.

Real audit (refute, 2026-05-02): mutation testing was considered and explicitly rejected as premature because the codebase is at 49.8% line coverage with risk-surface packages (LSP transport) at 0%. That rejection is correct but the skill never raised the question — the audit author had to surface and resolve it manually.

## Why it matters

Mutation testing measures *test discrimination*: how many code mutations the test suite catches. A 90%-coverage suite where 60% of mutants survive is worse than a 70%-coverage suite with 90% killed. Coverage-only audits silently endorse the first.

Tools: `gremlins` (active Go-native, sub-second per mutant on small packages), `go-mutesting` (older). For Go.

## Why P3

Mutation testing is a *force multiplier* on existing coverage, not a substitute. Recommending it on undertested code produces noise dominated by "untested is untested." The skill should recommend it **conditionally**, not universally.

## Suggested fix

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/SKILL.md`:

1. Add audit module "mutation testing" *after* the test-coverage gap module. Module is gated: applies only to packages with line coverage above a threshold (suggestion: 80%) AND classified as risk surface.
2. Module output: list of surviving mutants per package, not a percentage. Each surviving mutant in a risk-surface function → `warning`-level finding.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md`:

3. Add `### gremlins` entry. Run: `gremlins unleash --tags risk` (or per-package). Severity: surviving mutant in risk function → `warning`. Note runtime cost (minutes to hours per package).

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/scripts/audit-discover.py`:

4. Per-package coverage extraction (already done for the coverage module) gates the mutation-testing recommendation. Below threshold → emit "mutation testing premature; raise coverage first" message. Above threshold → emit gremlins recommendation.

## Acceptance

A regenerated audit for a Go repo with mixed-coverage packages emits gremlins recommendations only for packages that meet the coverage gate. Below-threshold packages get the "premature" message instead.

## Refute-specific note for the issue body

Refute should revisit mutation testing once `internal/edit/applier.go`, `internal/backend/lsp/transport.go`, and `parseWorkspaceEdit` cross 80% coverage. Likely candidates for surviving mutants today (if run anyway): operator mutations in `positionToOffset` line/character math, boundary mutations in `applyEdits` range checks.

title: generate-audit: recommend nilaway for Go repos with pointer-heavy decoding paths (with maturity caveat)
description:
## Gap

`catalog/skills/generate-audit/` does not mention `nilaway` (Uber's inter-procedural nil-pointer analyzer).

Real audit (refute, 2026-05-02): the LSP layer is full of pointer-shaped nilness — `jsonrpcResponse.ID *int`, `Error *jsonrpcError`, `CodeAction.Edit *json.RawMessage`. Each is optional, each is dereferenced after JSON unmarshal. A nilness bug would surface as a panic in the JSON-RPC reader where stderr is discarded (https://github.com/shatterproof-ai/refute/issues/6) and the user has zero diagnostic data.

## Why it matters

No other Go static analyzer does inter-procedural nilness tracking. `staticcheck`'s SA5011 catches some local cases; nilaway catches cross-function flow.

## Caveat

Uber markets nilaway as **experimental**. Higher false-positive rate than mature linters. If recommended without thought, the resulting `.golangci.yml` ends up papered with `// nilaway:ignore` annotations. The skill should recommend nilaway only when:

1. The repo has a high density of pointer-typed struct fields populated via JSON/protobuf decode, **and**
2. The repo has goroutine concurrency over those pointers, **and**
3. The maintainer accepts the experimental status.

## Suggested fix

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md`:

1. Add `### nilaway` entry. Run: `nilaway ./...` (separate invocation; check current golangci-lint integration status before recommending as a linter).
2. Severity: any finding → audit `error`. But mark the entire tool as `recommendation severity: medium` (not `high`) due to maturity.
3. Include a worked rejection criterion: "If nilaway produces > N false positives per KLOC, prefer focused review of pointer-decode sites instead."

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/scripts/audit-discover.py`:

4. Detect pointer-heavy decode signature: count Go struct fields of form `*T` where T is non-builtin in non-test files, and `json.Unmarshal` / `Unmarshal` call sites in the same package. Gate the recommendation on a threshold.

## Acceptance

A regenerated audit for a JSON-decode-heavy Go repo includes nilaway in the recommendations *with* its maturity caveat and the rejection criterion.

## Related

Split out from `bento-nzm` (golangci-lint config bundle).
close_reason: Dropped on review: nilaway is too narrow for skill-level recommendation. Pointer-decode-heavy + experimental tool with non-trivial false-positive rate. Maintainers of repos that would benefit from it tend to know they need it; the audit doesn't add the marginal nudge. Closing without implementation per the broadly-applicable rule.

title: generate-audit: add osv-scanner, actionlint, go-licenses, depcheck, madge
description:
## Gap

Five smaller-scope tools that the skill omits, each filling a specific blind spot.

## Per-tool fix

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/references/static-analysis-tools.md`, add:

1. **`### osv-scanner`** (Google) — modern alternative to `govulncheck` and `nancy`. Scans Go modules, npm, Maven, Cargo, requirements.txt in one pass against the OSV database. Run: `osv-scanner --recursive .`. Severity: any reachable vuln → `error`. Treat as alternative-to (not addition-to) `govulncheck` and `nancy` per "highest-value missing tool per gap" rule in `generation-rules.md`.

2. **`### actionlint`** — GitHub workflow linting. Run: `actionlint`. Triggered when `.github/workflows/` exists. Any error → `warning`. Surface as a finding any time the audit recommends adding CI but no `actionlint` is present in the post-CI plan.

3. **`### go-licenses`** — Google. License compliance for shipped Go binaries. Run: `go-licenses report ./cmd/...`. Treat any GPL/AGPL transitive dep in a non-GPL repo as `error`; missing license metadata as `warning`.

4. **`### depcheck`** (npm) — unused dependencies in TypeScript/JavaScript. Run: `npx depcheck`. Severity: unused dep → `note`; missing dep used in code → `error`. Complementary to `knip` which finds unused exports.

5. **`### madge`** (npm) — circular dependency detection in TypeScript/JavaScript. Run: `npx madge --circular --extensions ts,tsx,js,jsx src`. Any cycle → `warning`.

In `catalog/skills/generate-audit/scripts/audit-discover.py`:

6. Add all five to `_TOOL_PROBES` with appropriate language scopes and `missing_*` defaults.

## Why P3

Each is narrow-scope and individually optional. Bundled to avoid five tiny issues. Land after the higher-priority race/fuzz/semgrep/Java work.

## Acceptance

`audit-discover.py` reports each tool's presence/absence per the language scope above; static-analysis-tools.md has a run block for each; the highest-value-per-gap rule is preserved (osv-scanner replaces govulncheck/nancy when chosen, doesn't stack with them).
close_reason: Splitting: bundled five tools of mixed applicability. Refiling osv-scanner, actionlint, and depcheck as separate broadly-applicable issues. Dropping go-licenses (narrow to enterprise license-compliance use case) and madge (narrow to TS/JS, and circular imports rarely the audit's first finding). See replacement issues.

title: Optionally enrich bentobug reports with telemetry context
description: Add best-effort telemetry context to bentobug reports when recent relevant telemetry is available. This is optional enrichment only; reporting must proceed when telemetry is absent, disabled, empty, corrupt, or from a different session. See docs/plans/2026-04-26-bento-telemetry.md Issue B3.
acceptance_criteria: Tests cover telemetry present, telemetry absent, corrupt telemetry, and ambiguous multiple-skill context; telemetry is never a hard dependency for reporting.

title: Reconsider committing .launch-work/log.md; treat the breadcrumb as untracked working-tree state
description:
## Background

launch-work-log.py update --checkpoint <name> writes .launch-work/log.md AND commits it with chore(launch-work-log): <name>. The file's stated purpose (SKILL.md:144) is a crash-recovery breadcrumb so an interrupted task can be resumed.

A companion bug (bento-183) addresses the immediate leakage by requiring land-work-clean-log.py --apply before merge. This issue is the durable redesign question: does the breadcrumb need to be a tracked, committed file at all?

## Argument for untracking

Crash recovery only requires the file to persist on disk in the worktree across an agent's restart. Committing the file gains little:

- It does not survive worktree cleanup any better than an untracked file would.
- Cross-agent handoff within the same worktree works without a commit; the file is on disk.
- The only scenario where the commit matters is "push the branch to a remote and resume it on another machine" — vanishingly rare for these use cases.

Costs of committing:

- Every checkpoint generates a real commit, multiplying per-task commit count by ~5-6x.
- Branches diverge from main by N×checkpoints commits even before any real change.
- Two branches both running launch-work create merge conflicts on .launch-work/log.md when their rebase targets have ingested each other's logs (observed in the wild — see bento-183 reproducer).
- Requires the entire land-work-clean-log skill to undo every checkpoint at land time. The cleanup machinery exists only because the create side committed.

## Proposed change

Modify launch-work-log.py update to write .launch-work/log.md without git add / git commit. Add .launch-work/ to a recommended .gitignore template the install path can offer.

## Trade-offs

- Loses: cross-machine resumption of an in-progress branch via push/pull. Already minor — resumption requires the worktree, dev env, and agent runtime to be present.
- Loses: log being part of branch history if the worktree is destroyed without bento:closure. Mitigated by treating crashes as worktree-local.
- Gains: zero chore(launch-work-log) commits anywhere; merge math simplifies; land-work-clean-log.py becomes unnecessary; per-task commit count drops by ~5-6.

## Alternative if untracking is too aggressive

Compromise: write the log as a tracked file but squash all chore(launch-work-log): commits into a single sentinel commit that land-work-clean-log removes atomically as part of the merge. Still requires the cleanup skill but reduces commit count and makes the "did clean-log run?" check a single-commit lookup.

## Decision needed

Discussion. No action recommended until bento-183 (enforcement fix) lands. After that, weigh "fix it properly" vs "keep current model with enforcement".
notes: Closure evidence correction: implementation commit e44e26f is reachable from main; current main is 1a0d03963b3698a8b395955e8813f49f17820be9 and contains prior main 8045c38db34321ff136db4e5ee2f2ea150989f1d.
close_reason: 8045c38db34321ff136db4e5ee2f2ea150989f1d landed on main; includes e44e26f feat(launch-work): move progress log to ; never commit it

title: Extract PLUGIN_DEFS to a data file; reconsider generated abstract "screenshots"
description:
(1) Plugin composition lives in the `PLUGIN_DEFS` dict inside `scripts/build-plugins` (~lines 54-225), mixing composition data with ~150 lines of marketing metadata (palettes, brand colors, default prompts); `scripts/bump-plugin-versions` imports the build script as a module just to read it (~lines 28-35). Extract to a data file (e.g. `catalog/plugins.json`) that both scripts load; keeps composition reviewable as data. (2) ~150 lines of build-plugins (~572-734) hand-roll a PNG encoder drawing abstract gradient/rectangle images shipped as `screenshot-N.png` Codex marketplace metadata — fabricated screenshots presented as product screenshots; either drop them, replace with honest static assets, or label them as artwork.

Acceptance: bump-plugin-versions no longer imports build-plugins; build output unchanged (drift test green) apart from any screenshot decision; tests updated.

Post-review recommendation: default to dropping the generated screenshots unless the Codex marketplace schema requires screenshot entries; if required, keep them but name and label them as generated artwork (e.g. artwork-N.png plus a metadata label) so they are not mistaken for product screenshots.
